concepts.title: Gross Margin
concepts.short_definition: Revenue left after direct product costs, before operating expenses.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Gross margin tells you how much money a company keeps from each sale after paying for the product itself. Higher is usually healthier.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Gross margin = (Revenue − Cost of Goods Sold) ÷ Revenue. It measures pricing power and production efficiency before R&D, SG&A, and interest.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Watch gross-margin trajectory versus peers and mix. Expansion can signal pricing power or better mix; compression may mean cost inflation, discounting, or commodity exposure.
concepts.quant_explanation: GM = (Rev − COGS) / Rev. Contribution analysis often uses ΔGM × revenue to estimate earnings leverage from margin moves.

concepts.title: Free Cash Flow
concepts.short_definition: Cash left after operating needs and capital spending.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Free cash flow is the cash a business can use to pay debt, buy back stock, pay dividends, or reinvest — after keeping the business running.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Commonly FCF ≈ operating cash flow − capital expenditures. It is often more reliable than earnings when accruals are large.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Quality of FCF depends on working-capital swings and maintenance vs growth capex. Negative FCF can be fine for high-growth firms if returns on invested capital justify it.
concepts.quant_explanation: FCF yield = FCF / enterprise value (or market cap). Compare to WACC and peer yields.

concepts.title: Concentration Risk
concepts.short_definition: Too much portfolio value in one name, sector, or theme.
concepts.beginner_explanation: If one holding is a large share of your portfolio, a bad day for that stock becomes a bad day for you. That is concentration risk.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Concentration risk rises with position weight, correlated holdings, and shared factor exposures (e.g. several AI semis).
concepts.advanced_explanation: Measure single-name weight, sector weight, and pairwise correlation. Stress the book for a −20% move in the largest cluster.
concepts.quant_explanation: Herfindahl index H = Σ w_i². Higher H means more concentration. Marginal risk contribution ≈ w_i × β_i to the portfolio.

concepts.title: Volatility
concepts.short_definition: How much a price tends to move up and down over time.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Volatile stocks swing more. That can mean opportunity — and larger drawdowns.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Historical volatility is usually the standard deviation of returns. Implied volatility is the market’s forward-looking estimate from options prices.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Distinguish realized vs implied vol, and vol of vol. Regime shifts matter more than a single trailing window.
concepts.quant_explanation: σ_annual ≈ σ_daily × √252. Position sizing often scales as 1/σ for risk parity style budgets.

concepts.title: Operating Leverage
concepts.short_definition: How strongly profits move when sales change, given fixed costs.
concepts.beginner_explanation: If a company has high fixed costs, a small sales increase can boost profits a lot — and a sales drop can hurt a lot.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Operating leverage comes from fixed vs variable cost structure. High leverage amplifies both upside and downside.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Degree of operating leverage (DOL) ≈ %ΔEBIT / %ΔSales. Software and semis often show high DOL once capacity is built.
concepts.quant_explanation: DOL ≈ (Q(P−V)) / (Q(P−V)−F) where F is fixed cost, V variable cost per unit.

concepts.title: Pricing Power
concepts.short_definition: Ability to raise prices without losing much demand.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Companies with pricing power can charge more when costs rise and still keep customers.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Evidence includes sustained gross-margin resilience, brand strength, switching costs, and limited substitutes.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Test pricing power via elasticity of demand, competitor reactions, and whether price hikes stick after inflation cools.
concepts.quant_explanation: Rough check: ΔASP vs volume; margin bridge attributing price vs mix vs cost.

concepts.title: P/E Ratio
concepts.short_definition: Price investors pay for one dollar of earnings.
concepts.beginner_explanation: A P/E of 20 means you pay $20 for each $1 of annual earnings. Higher P/E can mean higher growth expectations — or an expensive stock.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Trailing P/E uses past earnings; forward P/E uses estimates. Compare to growth, peers, and history.
concepts.advanced_explanation: P/E can mislead with cyclical earnings, one-offs, or capital structure differences. Prefer EV/EBIT or FCF yield alongside.
concepts.quant_explanation: Justified P/E ≈ payout / (r − g) in a Gordon-style model when assumptions hold.

concepts.title: EV/EBITDA
concepts.short_definition: Enterprise value relative to cash operating earnings.
concepts.beginner_explanation: EV/EBITDA compares the whole-firm value (equity + net debt) to a rough operating profit measure. Useful for comparing companies with different debt levels.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Enterprise value = equity market cap + net debt (+ other claims). EBITDA is earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Watch for lease capitalization, stock-based compensation, and capex intensity — EBITDA ignores maintenance investment.
concepts.quant_explanation: EV/EBITDA = EV / EBITDA. Cross-check with EV/EBIT and FCF conversion (FCF/EBITDA).

concepts.title: Drawdown
concepts.short_definition: Peak-to-trough decline in value.
concepts.beginner_explanation: If your portfolio fell from $100k to $80k, that is a 20% drawdown — even if it later recovers.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Max drawdown is the worst peak-to-trough loss in a period. It measures pain better than volatility alone.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Path dependency matters: sequence of returns and rebalancing change experienced drawdowns.
concepts.quant_explanation: DD_t = (P_t − max_{s≤t} P_s) / max_{s≤t} P_s. MaxDD = min DD_t.

concepts.title: Beta
concepts.short_definition: How much a stock tends to move with the market.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Beta near 1 moves with the market. Above 1 usually swings more; below 1 usually swings less.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Beta is estimated from historical returns versus a benchmark. It is unstable and regime-dependent.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Distinguish raw, adjusted, and fundamental beta. High-beta clusters amplify book risk in risk-off regimes.
concepts.quant_explanation: β = Cov(r_i, r_m) / Var(r_m). Portfolio β ≈ Σ w_i β_i.

concepts.title: Investment Thesis
concepts.short_definition: Your reasoned case for owning (or avoiding) a position.
concepts.beginner_explanation: A thesis is the story and evidence behind a decision — not just a ticker tip.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: A good thesis states the claim, key evidence, catalysts, risks, and what would prove you wrong.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Living theses should version over time with evidence links and explicit invalidation conditions.
concepts.quant_explanation: Track conviction as rating/confidence history and map catalysts to dated checkpoints.

concepts.title: Catalyst
concepts.short_definition: An event that could change the investment story soon.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Earnings, a product launch, a court ruling, or a rate decision can be catalysts — things that may move the stock when they happen.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Catalysts should be specific and dated when possible. Weak theses lean on vague ‘momentum’ without events.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Separate priced-in vs surprise catalysts. Position sizing should reflect binary outcomes.
concepts.quant_explanation: Scenario tree: P(outcome) × Δvaluation under each branch; expected value vs current price.

concepts.title: Invalidation Condition
concepts.short_definition: A clear signal that the thesis is broken.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Decide in advance what would make you change your mind — for example, losing a key customer or a margin collapse.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Invalidation conditions turn opinions into testable claims and reduce sunk-cost bias.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Prefer measurable triggers (gross margin < X, net retention < Y) over narrative vibes.
concepts.quant_explanation: Map each condition to a monitoring rule and alert threshold.

concepts.title: Evidence Quality
concepts.short_definition: How trustworthy and relevant a source is for a claim.
concepts.beginner_explanation: A primary filing usually beats a random social post. Better evidence makes better decisions.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Score evidence by provider authority, recency, specificity, and whether it actually supports the claim.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Separate source quality from claim strength. Unsupported numerical claims are a red flag.
concepts.quant_explanation: Composite score = w1·authority + w2·freshness + w3·specificity, normalized to [0,1].

concepts.title: Look-Ahead Bias
concepts.short_definition: Using information that was not knowable at the decision time.
concepts.beginner_explanation: If you judge a past decision using today’s knowledge, you are cheating the timeline.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Backtests and research reviews must respect as-of dates for prices, filings, and news.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Point-in-time databases and filing timestamps are required for honest historical evaluation.
concepts.quant_explanation: Enforce trade_date cutoff: discard evidence with published_at > trade_date.

concepts.title: Position Sizing
concepts.short_definition: How large a holding should be relative to the portfolio.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Sizing decides how much you can win — and how much you can lose — on one idea.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Common approaches: equal weight, conviction tiers, volatility targeting, and max single-name caps.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Size should reflect edge, uncertainty, liquidity, and correlation with the rest of the book.
concepts.quant_explanation: Kelly fraction f* = edge/odds (use fractional Kelly). Risk-budget: w_i ∝ 1/σ_i subject to Σw=1 and caps.

concepts.title: Diversification
concepts.short_definition: Spreading risk across imperfectly correlated assets.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Don’t put everything in one stock. Different holdings can cushion each other.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: True diversification needs low correlation, not just more tickers in the same theme.
concepts.advanced_explanation: In crises, correlations often rise. Diversify across factors and regimes, not just names.
concepts.quant_explanation: Portfolio variance σ_p² = Σ_i Σ_j w_i w_j Cov_ij. Diversification ratio = weighted σ / σ_p.

concepts.title: Economic Moat
concepts.short_definition: A durable advantage that protects profits from competitors.
concepts.beginner_explanation: A moat is why a good business stays good — brand, network effects, switching costs, or cost advantage.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Assess moat width and durability. Moats erode; re-underwrite them when competition or regulation shifts.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Map moat to ROIC persistence and reinvestment runway. Narrow moats may still be great at the right price.
concepts.quant_explanation: Track ROIC − WACC spread over time and fade assumptions in DCF.

concepts.title: Company Guidance
concepts.short_definition: Management’s forward outlook for key metrics.
concepts.beginner_explanation: Guidance is what the company says it expects next — often revenue, margins, or EPS.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: Compare guidance vs consensus, prior guide, and historical accuracy (sandbagging vs optimism).
concepts.advanced_explanation: Revisions and midpoint moves matter more than a single print. Watch for qualitative tone shifts.
concepts.quant_explanation: Δguide vs street; implied beat/miss distribution around the midpoint.

concepts.title: SEC Filing
concepts.short_definition: Official company disclosure filed with the U.S. regulator.
concepts.beginner_explanation: 10-K, 10-Q, and 8-K filings are primary sources. Prefer them over secondhand summaries when stakes are high.
concepts.intermediate_explanation: 10-K annual, 10-Q quarterly, 8-K material events. MD&A and risk factors are high-signal sections.
concepts.advanced_explanation: Use XBRL companyfacts for structured line items; always keep the filing URL as evidence.
concepts.quant_explanation: Join facts by taxonomy tag + period; never mix as-reported and restated without flags.
